narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can take on right now—it's like piecing together a story from the chapters of your academic journey so far. At the heart of it, your Year 3 decile of 2.0 stood out as the biggest signal, whispering that even with your full-time commitment ahead, the competition in law school might pull those grades down further, much like it did from your Year 1 decile of 3.0. Your LSAT score of 29 then layered on real concern, painting a picture of someone who might struggle to keep pace with the intense reasoning demands of bar prep. Those early undergrad signals, like your 3.0 UGPA, added to the worry that foundational habits might not fully bridge the gap. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket made the model nervous too, as it often means fewer resources for the extra tutoring or support that turns close calls into passes. Your background as a Black male applicant tugged in the same direction, reflecting patterns we've seen where systemic hurdles stack up against the odds. Even being full-time and targeting law school full-heartedly couldn't outweigh these combined forces, which together gave you a 68% predicted chance of not passing the bar—it's not about your potential or drive, but the mosaic of these past markers that made the model cautious. This isn't the end of your story, though; many have rewritten theirs by bolstering those weak spots first. You've got heart—use it to target LSAT retakes or grade-boosting strategies before reapplying. We're rooting for your comeback.
